one_line_takeaway: By progressively masking image patches and clustering the resulting embedding trajectory with DBSCAN, BackdoorIDS detects backdoored vision-encoder inputs purely from a single forward-pass sequence at inference time -- no training data, auxiliary set, or retraining required -- and beats prior zero/low-knowledge detectors (PatchProcessing, DeDe) in TPR across five attack/encoder combinations including CNNs, ViTs, CLIP, and LLaVA-1.5.
key_finding: BackdoorIDS achieves the highest TPR of the three inference-phase detectors on every attack tested, with the lowest FPR on four of five settings (Table 1): BadEncoder TPR 95.78% / FPR 17.38% (vs. PatchProcessing 84.32%/22.73% and DeDe 89.01%/18.50%); Drupe 82.75%/19.18% (vs. 74.66%/25.13% and 69.94%/20.94%); BadCLIP-RN 93.08%/28.38% (vs. 63.11%/31.84% and 70.20%/25.26%, the one case where BackdoorIDS's FPR is not the lowest); BadCLIP-ViT 95.84%/25.76% (vs. 82.97%/26.78% and 55.12%/27.97%); BadVision 97.00%/23.78% (vs. 41.00%/27.54% and DeDe collapsing to 7.00%/29.68%). Downstream-impact results (Table 4) show BackdoorIDS drives ASR far lower than purification baselines (ZIP, SampDetox) and detection baseline DeDe -- e.g. ASR of 4.53% on BadEncoder, 7.59% on Drupe, 6.92% on BadCLIP-RN, 4.12% on BadCLIP-ViT, and 3.00% on BadVision, versus No-defense ASRs of 99.48%, 83.05%, 99.61%, 99.99%, and 99.00% respectively -- at the cost of somewhat reduced Clean Accuracy relative to purification methods (e.g. CA 65.69% on BadEncoder vs. ZIP's 62.15% and SampDetox's 63.52%, but CA 42.49% on BadCLIP-RN vs. SampDetox's 48.98%). Runtime per image (Table 2) is close to DeDe and much faster than PatchProcessing on the costly LVLM setting (BadVision: 3.225s for BackdoorIDS vs. 0.994s for DeDe vs. 15.113s for PatchProcessing); the authors note this is only a 32% overhead relative to LLaVA-1.5's own 9.879s inference time. A diagnostic breakdown against Drupe (Table 3) shows that most of BackdoorIDS's false negatives (samples with ASR only 48.98%, vs. 89.51% for true positives) correspond to backdoor samples whose trigger largely fails to activate the backdoor anyway, so effective backdoor coverage after defense (normalized 'Whole' ASR) is only 7.59%. Sensitivity analyses show performance is fairly robust to s, k, patch grid size, masking ratio (0.5-0.9), and masking-trajectory random seed (std of TPR/FPR across 10 seeds under 0.5 percentage points), and to JPEG compression down to quality 40, though FPR is not lowest against BadCLIP-RN and detection on BadCLIP-ViT degrades sharply once JPEG quality drops below 40.
core_contribution: The paper proposes BackdoorIDS, a zero-shot, inference-time detector that flags whether a single input image is a backdoor sample for a (possibly compromised) pretrained vision encoder, without needing the encoder's original training data, any auxiliary/OOD dataset, retraining, or fine-tuning. It is motivated by two mechanistic observations the authors claim to be new: 'Attention Hijacking' (once a trigger is active, encoder attention is almost entirely absorbed by trigger features and benign content is nearly ignored, for both patch-based and blended/distributed triggers) and 'Attention Restoration' (once progressive patch masking removes enough of the trigger to deactivate it, attention abruptly snaps back to benign content, producing a sharp embedding jump, whereas clean images show smooth, gradual embedding drift under masking). Operationally: the image is divided into a 16x16 patch grid; patches are masked in random order to produce a sequence of increasingly masked images (only the first 3/4 of the sequence is used); each masked image is passed through the vision encoder to yield an embedding sequence; the average pairwise cosine-distance local density of the top-k (default k=5) earliest embeddings is computed and multiplied by a scaling factor s to set a DBSCAN clustering radius (s=5 for encoders trained from scratch such as ResNet, s=50 for large-scale pretrained encoders such as CLIP/LLaVA-1.5); DBSCAN is run over the full embedding sequence with min_samples=k and cosine metric; if the sequence collapses into a single cluster the input is called clean, if it splits into more than one cluster it is called backdoor.
threat_model: Defender has black-box, inference-time access only to the test input image and the (potentially backdoored) vision encoder's forward pass (to obtain embeddings) -- no access to the encoder's original pretraining data, no auxiliary/OOD dataset, no labels, and no ability to retrain or fine-tune the encoder. The adversary is assumed to have already backdoored the vision encoder upstream (e.g., poisoning web-scale pretraining data or releasing a compromised third-party encoder), embedding a trigger that, when present in an input, steers the encoder's embedding/output toward an attacker-chosen target regardless of the encoder's downstream use (classification, captioning, or as the vision tower in an LVLM). Triggers considered include simple fixed color patches (BadEncoder, Drupe), optimized adaptive patch triggers (BadCLIP), and optimized blended (distributed, image-wide) triggers (BadVision). The defender's goal is per-sample detection at inference time to filter out backdoor-triggered inputs before they reach the downstream task.
limitations: Explicitly acknowledged/evident in the paper: (1) FPR is not always the best among compared methods -- on BadCLIP-RN, BackdoorIDS's 28.38% FPR is worse than DeDe's 25.26%, despite the higher TPR. (2) Detection performance depends on hand-set, dataset/architecture-dependent hyperparameters (scaling factor s=5 for from-scratch encoders vs. s=50 for large pretrained encoders; k=5; 16x16 grid; 3/4 masking-sequence truncation) chosen empirically rather than derived analytically or set adaptively per-input. (3) Robustness degrades under stronger JPEG compression (quality <40) for BadCLIP-ViT, where the method's ability to distinguish backdoor from clean collapses. (4) The method incurs a non-trivial extra inference cost (multiple forward passes per image along the masking trajectory), which the paper argues is acceptable (32% overhead for LVLM captioning) but is nonzero, and scales with the number of masking steps evaluated. (5) Clean Accuracy after filtering is generally lower than that of purification-based defenses (ZIP, SampDetox), since flagged-and-discarded clean samples directly reduce CA -- the paper frames this as an acceptable security/utility trade-off but does not eliminate it. (6) Evaluation is confined to four specific attack families (BadEncoder, Drupe, BadCLIP, BadVision) and does not test against attacks explicitly designed to be robust to patch-masking perturbations or adaptive attackers aware of the detection mechanism; no adaptive-attack evaluation is presented.

datasets_used: CIFAR-10 (pretraining/poisoning set for BadEncoder and Drupe)
CGD subset of MIMIC-IT (pretraining/poisoning set for BadVision)
STL-10 (used as the OOD auxiliary set for the DeDe baseline)
quantitative_results: TPR/FPR (Table 1): BadEncoder 95.78/17.38, Drupe 82.75/19.18, BadCLIP(RN) 93.08/28.38, BadCLIP(ViT) 95.84/25.76, BadVision 97.00/23.78 for BackdoorIDS
PatchProcessing TPR/FPR: BadEncoder 84.32/22.73, Drupe 74.66/25.13, BadCLIP(RN) 63.11/31.84, BadCLIP(ViT) 82.97/26.78, BadVision 41.00/27.54
DeDe TPR/FPR: BadEncoder 89.01/18.50, Drupe 69.94/20.94, BadCLIP(RN) 70.20/25.26, BadCLIP(ViT) 55.12/27.97, BadVision 7.00/29.68

Runtime per image in seconds (Table 2): BackdoorIDS 0.031 (BadEncoder), 0.032 (Drupe), 0.219 (BadCLIP-RN), 0.201 (BadCLIP-ViT), 3.225 (BadVision), vs. DeDe 0.024/0.025/0.113/0.104/0.994 and PatchProcessing 0.082/0.084/0.932/0.883/15.113

ASR/CA with BackdoorIDS (Table 4): BadEncoder 4.53/65.69, Drupe 7.59/55.95, BadCLIP(RN) 6.92/42.49, BadCLIP(ViT) 4.12/46.92, BadVision 3.00/68.68(*CIDEr); No-defense ASR/CA: 99.48/79.11, 83.05/74.99, 99.61/57.48, 99.99/61.39, 99.00/85.30
Drupe diagnostic (Table 3): TP=523, FN=109, TN=9860, FP=2138; ASR on TP=89.51%, ASR on FN=48.98%, normalized 'Whole' ASR after defense=7.59%, Whole CA=55.95%
8x8 vs 16x16 grid ablation (Table 5): 16x16 gives BadEncoder TPR/FPR 95.78/17.38 and BadCLIP(ViT) 95.84/25.76; 8x8 gives 91.26/12.74 and 86.4/6.67

Masking-trajectory stability across 10 seeds (Table 6): BadEncoder TPR mean 95.70 (std 0.46, range 94.70-96.24), FPR mean 17.63 (std 0.14); BadCLIP(ViT) TPR mean 95.73 (std 0.44), FPR mean 25.73 (std 0.11)

Poisoned test-set composition: SVHN 1,302 backdoor / 24,730 clean; GTSRB 632 backdoor / 11,998 clean; ImageNet-1K 2,500 backdoor / 47,500 clean; COCO Caption 100 backdoor / 1,900 clean (all at 5% poison rate)
baselines_compared: PatchProcessing (patch drop/shuffle + entropy-based detection, encoder analogue of STRIP)
DeDe (decoder trained on OOD/auxiliary data, detects via reconstruction error)
